reasoning: I will provide a concise summary of the article, highlighting the key facts, figures, and quotes about homelessness among ex-prisoners in Wales, housing policy changes, and responses from charities and the government.
output: The article reports that Prison Link Cymru handled 1,099 referrals in 2015-16 and notes some ex-offenders remain homeless for up to a year after release. It argues that investing in housing would be cheaper than jailing repeat offenders, while the Welsh Government says more people are being helped with housing. Changes to Wales’ Housing Act in 2015 removed the right of prison leavers to priority accommodation. The charity says women fare better because needs like children and domestic violence are prioritized, whereas men’s issues (PTSD, drug dependency) are often deprioritized. Andrew Stevens describes accommodation needs as chronic, with six months to a year often elapsing before securing housing. He suggests building more one-bedroom flats could reduce costs to the public purse. Official data show 830 one-bedroom properties were built in the year to March 2016, out of 6,900 total new homes in Wales. Marc, a long-time ex-offender, struggled to find accommodation but has since found stability with Emmaus, which Tom Clarke of Emmaus South Wales says is not a one-size-fits-all solution and more services are needed. A Welsh Government spokesman defended the national pathway for homeless services in the secure estate and noted demand for one-bedroom flats, alongside plans to deliver 20,000 new affordable homes in five years.
